title: Eggless Tiramisu
description: A no-egg, no-alcohol version of Italy's most beloved layered dessert — coffee-soaked ladyfinger biscuits stacked between clouds of mascarpone cream, finished with a generous dusting of cocoa. Rich, make-ahead, and reliably impressive.

ingredients.name: ladyfinger biscuits (savoiardi)
ingredients.notes: or any dry sponge biscuit; Marie biscuits also work

ingredients.name: mascarpone cheese
ingredients.notes: or full-fat cream cheese (Philadelphia-style)

ingredients.name: sweetened condensed milk
ingredients.notes: adjust to taste; replaces the sugar and egg yolks

steps.text: Heat 200 ml water in a small saucepan until steaming. Remove from heat, add instant coffee powder and sugar, and stir until both dissolve. Set aside and allow to cool completely to room temperature — hot coffee will turn the biscuits soggy instantly.

Pour the heavy cream into a chilled bowl and whip with a hand whisk or electric beater until it holds soft, pillowy peaks — it should be thick enough to hold shape but not grainy. Refrigerate while you prepare the mascarpone base.
Beat the mascarpone in a separate bowl until smooth and lump-free. Add condensed milk and vanilla extract and mix well until the mixture is creamy and uniform.
Gently fold the whipped cream into the mascarpone mixture using a spatula, using large sweeping movements to keep the cream airy. The combined mixture should be thick, pale, and hold soft peaks.

Dip each ladyfinger biscuit into the cooled coffee for 1–2 seconds per side — they should be soaked through but still hold their shape. Arrange them in a single snug layer at the base of a 20×15 cm dish or individual glasses.
Spread half the cream mixture evenly over the soaked biscuit layer, smoothing it to the edges with a spatula.
Add a second layer of coffee-dipped biscuits on top, then spread the remaining cream mixture over it and smooth the surface flat.

Sift a generous layer of cocoa powder evenly over the entire top surface. Cover the dish tightly with cling film and refrigerate for at least 4 hours — overnight is ideal, as the layers meld and the dessert slices cleanly.
Serve cold, dusting with a little extra cocoa just before bringing to the table. Leftovers keep covered in the refrigerator for up to 2 days.

substitutions.substitute: 250 g thick hung curd mixed with 2 tbsp malai — lighter and slightly tangy
substitutions.notes: drain curd in muslin for 4 hours first to remove whey

substitutions.substitute: chilled coconut cream whipped to soft peaks — gives a mild coconut note
4 tbsp icing sugar — start with 3 and taste before adding the last spoon
plain tea biscuits (Marie biscuits) or Britannia Good Day cut to fit the dish — they soak faster, so reduce dip time to 1 second

culturalNote: Tiramisù — literally 'lift me up' in Italian — is believed to have originated in the Veneto region and became one of the most replicated desserts worldwide; eggless adaptations are popular across South and Southeast Asia where raw eggs in desserts are less common.
indianKitchenAdaptation: No kadai or pressure cooker required — this is a pure assembly dessert. Amul fresh cream whips reliably when well chilled; cream cheese is available at metro supermarkets or substitute thick hung curd. Marie biscuits or Britannia Nice biscuits stand in for savoiardi. Use Nescafé or Bru instant coffee powder for the soak.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Pressure-Cooker Chocolate Cake
description: A moist, deeply chocolatey sponge baked without an oven — the classic Indian home-baker trick of using a dry-heat pressure cooker produces a tender crumb indistinguishable from an oven cake. Foolproof, budget-friendly, and endlessly customisable.

ingredients.name: butter, melted and cooled
ingredients.notes: or neutral oil

ingredients.name: coarse salt or clean sand (for pressure-cooker base)
ingredients.notes: creates the dry heat environment; never replaced by water

steps.text: Spread 2 cups of coarse salt or clean dry sand in the pressure cooker base. Place a small steel stand or a ring of crumpled foil inside to elevate the cake tin. Close the lid WITHOUT the gasket and WITHOUT the whistle weight — this is a dry-heat method, not steaming. Heat on medium flame for 10 minutes to preheat.
Sift together the maida, cocoa powder, baking powder, baking soda, and a pinch of salt into a bowl. Whisk to combine and set aside.
In a larger bowl, whisk the sugar, eggs, melted butter, milk, and vanilla extract together until smooth, pale, and well combined — about 1–2 minutes.

Add the sifted dry ingredients to the wet mixture in two batches. Fold gently with a spatula until just combined — a few lumps are fine. Stop mixing the moment no dry streaks remain; overmixing toughens the crumb.

Grease a 15 cm (6-inch) round steel or aluminium cake tin with oil or butter and dust lightly with flour or cocoa powder. Pour in the batter — it should fill the tin no more than two-thirds full to allow rise.
Carefully place the filled tin on the stand inside the preheated pressure cooker. Close the lid without the gasket and without the whistle — you need dry heat circulation, not steam pressure.

Bake on medium-low flame for 35–40 minutes. Begin checking at the 30-minute mark by inserting a wooden toothpick or thin skewer into the centre — it should come out with only dry crumbs, not wet batter.
Turn off the flame and let the cake rest inside the closed cooker for 5 minutes. Then carefully lift the tin out using tongs or folded kitchen cloths.

Cool in the tin for 10 minutes on a rack. Run a knife around the edge and unmould onto a plate. The cake should release cleanly and the top should be set, dry, and spring back when pressed. Serve warm as is, or cool completely before frosting with whipped cream or a dusting of icing sugar.

substitutions.substitute: 4 tbsp neutral cooking oil — the cake is equally moist, just slightly denser
125 ml thick curd thinned with a splash of water — adds a subtle tang and keeps the crumb tender
3/4 cup wheat flour (atta) for a nuttier, slightly denser cake; do not swap 1:1 — reduce by a quarter
3 tbsp drinking chocolate powder (Bournvita or Milo) — the cake will be sweeter and less intensely chocolatey; reduce sugar by 2 tbsp

culturalNote: The pressure-cooker cake method became a practical innovation in Indian kitchens where ovens were not standard; generations of home bakers have used this technique to produce celebration cakes without any specialised equipment.
indianKitchenAdaptation: This recipe IS the Indian kitchen adaptation — sand or salt base, no gasket, no whistle. Use Amul butter or refined sunflower oil. Bru or Cadbury cocoa powder work well. If you do have an OTG or oven, preheat to 175°C and bake in the greased tin on the middle rack for 25–30 minutes instead.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Banana Bread
description: A tender, fragrant quick bread packed with the sweetness of overripe bananas — dense enough to feel like a treat, yet simple enough for a weekday bake. The darker and softer the bananas, the more flavourful the loaf.

ingredients.name: very ripe bananas (peeled, mashed)
ingredients.notes: skins should be heavily speckled or black — the riper, the sweeter

ingredients.name: butter, melted
ingredients.notes: about 5 tbsp; or neutral oil

steps.text: Preheat oven to 175°C (350°F). Grease a standard 23×13 cm loaf tin with butter or oil and line the base with a strip of parchment paper.
Peel the bananas and mash them thoroughly in a large bowl with a fork until almost smooth — a few small lumps are welcome as they create pockets of soft banana in the finished loaf.
Stir the melted butter into the mashed banana. Add sugar, beaten eggs, and vanilla extract and mix until the batter is uniform and slightly glossy.
Sprinkle the baking soda and salt over the batter and stir to distribute evenly.

Add the maida all at once. Fold gently with a spatula until just incorporated — the batter will look shaggy and lumpy; this is correct. Stop folding the moment no dry flour streaks remain, as overmixing develops gluten and results in a rubbery loaf.
Fold in the walnuts if using, then pour the batter into the prepared tin and smooth the top lightly.

Bake for 55–60 minutes until the top is deeply golden-brown and cracked down the centre, and a toothpick inserted in the middle comes out with only moist crumbs — not wet batter. Check at 50 minutes; ovens vary.
Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then lift out by the parchment lining and transfer to a wire rack. Slice only once the loaf is fully cool — a warm loaf will compress and tear.

substitutions.substitute: 1/3 cup coconut oil or neutral vegetable oil — equally moist
2 tbsp thick curd per egg for an eggless version — the loaf will be slightly denser but still delicious
50 g dark chocolate chips or 3 tbsp desiccated coconut for an egg-free version without nut allergies
1.5 cups whole wheat flour (atta) for a nuttier, fibre-rich loaf; add 2 extra tbsp milk to compensate for the drier flour

culturalNote: Banana bread became a staple of the American home kitchen during the Depression era when bakers looked for ways to use overripe fruit that would otherwise be discarded; it remains one of the most searched baking recipes globally.
regionalVariation: Hawaiian variations add macadamia nuts and coconut; Southern US versions often include pecans and a brown-sugar crumble topping.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Bake in an OTG at 175°C, or use the pressure-cooker tin method: preheat with salt/sand base (no gasket, no whistle) for 10 minutes on medium, then bake on low flame for 65–70 minutes. Use Amul butter or refined oil. Very ripe 'rasthali' or 'robusta' bananas sold cheaply at the market are ideal — the darker and softer the skin, the sweeter the bread.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Arroz con Leche (Spanish Rice Pudding)
description: Creamy Spanish rice pudding simmered low and slow with whole milk, cinnamon, and lemon zest until the grains are silky-soft and the custard-like sauce clings to every spoonful. Served warm in winter or chilled in summer, it is the most universal comfort dessert of the Spanish-speaking world.

ingredients.name: short-grain or regular white rice
ingredients.notes: rinse well; short-grain gives the creamiest result

ingredients.name: whole milk
ingredients.notes: full-fat gives the richest texture

ingredients.name: lemon (zest only, in strips)
ingredients.notes: use a vegetable peeler; avoid the white pith

steps.text: Rinse the rice in cold water until the water runs nearly clear. Drain well.

Pour the milk into a heavy-bottomed saucepan. Add the cinnamon stick, lemon zest strips, and a pinch of salt. Place on medium heat and bring to a gentle simmer, stirring occasionally to prevent the milk from forming a skin or scorching on the base.

Add the rinsed rice to the simmering milk. Reduce the flame to its lowest setting and cook uncovered, stirring every 3–4 minutes with a wooden spoon to prevent sticking, for 35–40 minutes until the rice is very soft and the mixture has thickened to a loose, creamy porridge-like consistency.

Add the sugar and stir until fully dissolved. Taste and add more sugar if desired. The pudding will thicken considerably as it cools, so remove from heat while it still looks a little looser than you want it.
Remove and discard the cinnamon stick and lemon zest strips. Stir in vanilla extract if using.
Spoon into individual bowls or glasses. Serve warm with a dusting of ground cinnamon on top, or press cling film onto the surface and refrigerate for 2 hours for a chilled version.

substitutions.substitute: a mix of 300 ml full-fat coconut milk and 500 ml regular milk for a tropical note
4 tbsp condensed milk stirred in at the end — richly sweet and thickens the pudding further
1 pod of green cardamom (lightly crushed) for an Indian-spiced version; remove before serving
short-grain sona masoori rice or even basmati works well; cooking time stays the same

culturalNote: Arroz con leche is among the oldest European desserts, appearing in cookbooks across Spain since the medieval period; in the twentieth century it spread through every Spanish-speaking country, each adapting it to local ingredients and sweetness levels.
regionalVariation: Mexican and Colombian versions use condensed and evaporated milk for added richness and a creamier, sweeter result; Peruvian arroz con leche often includes raisins and coconut.
indianKitchenAdaptation: This is essentially a relative of kheer or payasam — use the same technique on a kadai over the lowest flame, stirring regularly. Full-fat cow or buffalo milk gives the richest result. Sona Masoori or ponni rice works perfectly. Serve chilled in small steel bowls dusted with cinnamon — it will feel pleasantly familiar to anyone who has eaten rice kheer.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Churros with Chocolate Dipping Sauce
description: Crisp, ridged fried dough sticks rolled in cinnamon sugar and served with a thick, glossy dark chocolate dipping sauce — Spain's most iconic street-food breakfast and the ultimate teatime indulgence. The dough takes five minutes, the frying ten; only the eating takes longer.

ingredients.name: dark chocolate (70% cocoa) or cocoa powder
ingredients.notes: Morde or Cadbury dark chocolate works well

steps.text: Make the chocolate sauce first so it stays warm: heat milk in a small saucepan over low flame until it just simmers. Add broken dark chocolate pieces and butter. Stir constantly off and on the heat until the chocolate melts into a smooth, glossy sauce. Keep on the lowest flame possible.

Make the churro dough: bring 1 cup of water to a rolling boil in a medium saucepan. Add salt, then tip in the maida all at once. Remove from heat and stir vigorously with a wooden spoon until the dough comes together and pulls cleanly away from the sides of the pan — about 1 minute of energetic stirring.
Cool the dough for 3–4 minutes until it is warm rather than hot and you can comfortably touch it.
Mix the sugar and ground cinnamon together in a shallow plate for coating. Set near the hob.

Heat oil in a deep kadai to 170–180°C. To test the temperature without a thermometer, drop a pea-sized piece of dough into the oil — it should sizzle immediately and rise to the surface within 2 seconds.

Transfer the churro dough into a piping bag fitted with a large star tip, or into a sturdy zip-lock bag with a 1 cm corner snipped off. Pipe 12–15 cm lengths of dough directly into the hot oil, cutting each one off with scissors. Fry 3–4 churros at a time — do not crowd the pan.
Fry for 3–4 minutes, turning once with tongs, until the churros are uniformly deep golden-brown on all sides and cooked through. Lift out and drain briefly on kitchen paper.

While still hot, toss the churros in the cinnamon-sugar mixture until coated all over. Serve immediately with the warm chocolate sauce on the side. Churros soften quickly — eat them straight from the pan.

substitutions.substitute: 3/4 cup maida + 1/4 cup rice flour for an extra-crisp exterior
100 ml coconut milk for a dairy-free chocolate sauce — the coconut note pairs beautifully with dark chocolate
100 g milk chocolate for a sweeter, milder dipping sauce; reduce or omit the butter
1 tbsp coconut oil for a dairy-free sauce

culturalNote: Churros are the quintessential morning street food of Spain, traditionally eaten at a churrería with a thick cup of hot chocolate; the name likely comes from the churra sheep, whose ribbed horns the fritter vaguely resembles.
regionalVariation: In Mexico and across Latin America, churros are often filled with caramel (cajeta), dulce de leche, or cream; Mexican churros tend to be shorter and thicker than the thin Madrid-style sticks.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A heavy kadai is the ideal vessel for deep-frying — the same setup used for murukku or jalebi. Oil temperature is key; use the wooden-spoon bubble test if you lack a thermometer. Star-shaped piping nozzles (chakri nozzle) are sold at baking supply shops across most Indian cities. Use Morde 70% dark compound or Cadbury dark for the sauce. Serve immediately — churros do not wait.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Steamed Chocolate Pudding
description: A proper British steamed pudding — intensely chocolatey, impossibly moist, with a dense almost fudgy crumb that no oven cake can replicate. Steamed over simmering water for a gentle, even cook, it arrives at the table trembling with richness and calls loudly for a pour of warm custard.

steps.text: Grease a 900 ml heatproof pudding bowl or a 15 cm round steel tin generously with butter, then dust with cocoa powder. Cut a circle of parchment paper to fit the base and press it in.

Beat softened butter and sugar together with a hand beater or wooden spoon until pale and fluffy — about 3 minutes of brisk beating. This creaming step is key to a light pudding despite the long steam.
Add the eggs one at a time, beating well after each. If the mixture looks curdled, beat in 1 tbsp of the flour and it will come back together.

Sift together the maida, cocoa powder, baking powder, and salt. Fold into the butter mixture alternately with the milk, in two additions each. Stir in vanilla extract. The batter should be thick and glossy, falling from the spoon in ribbons.

Spoon the batter into the prepared bowl and smooth the top. Cover tightly with a double layer of greased parchment or foil, pressing a pleat into the centre to allow for rise. Secure tightly around the rim with kitchen string or crimp the foil.

Place the bowl on a trivet or upturned small plate inside a large pot. Pour boiling water to come halfway up the side of the bowl. Cover the pot with its lid and bring the water to a steady simmer over medium flame.

Steam for 45–50 minutes on a steady simmer, checking every 15 minutes and topping up with boiling water if the level drops. The pudding is done when a skewer inserted through the foil comes out with moist — not wet — crumbs.

Carefully lift the bowl using tongs. Remove the foil, run a knife around the edge, and invert the pudding onto a deep plate. Serve immediately with warm custard, whipped cream, or a scoop of vanilla ice cream.

substitutions.substitute: 100 g coconut oil for a dairy-free version — the pudding will be slightly less rich
2 tbsp thick curd per egg for an eggless adaptation — texture will be a little denser but still moist
3 tbsp coconut milk for a dairy-free, tropical-chocolate note
3/4 cup wheat flour (atta) for a nuttier, slightly denser pudding

culturalNote: Steamed puddings have been central to British winter cooking since the eighteenth century, when the technique of steaming in a cloth or basin over simmering water predated widespread domestic ovens; the gentle heat produces a texture baking simply cannot match.
regionalVariation: The classic British Christmas pudding is a dense, dark steamed fruit pudding steeped in alcohol; this chocolate version is a lighter everyday cousin.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Use a pressure cooker as your steamer: place the filled bowl on a trivet inside, pour in 2 cups of water, and close the lid WITH the gasket but WITHOUT the whistle weight so steam escapes gently. Cook on medium-low for 45 minutes. Check occasionally and add hot water if the level drops. Amul butter and standard cocoa powder work perfectly.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: No-Bake Mango Cheesecake
description: A lush, no-oven cheesecake on a buttery biscuit base — the cream-cheese filling is lifted with ripe mango pulp and set with agar-agar for a clean, silky slice that tastes purely of summer. Make it the evening before and unmould straight from the refrigerator.

ingredients.name: digestive or Marie biscuits
ingredients.notes: Britannia Digestive or Marie biscuits are widely available

ingredients.name: cream cheese (full-fat)
ingredients.notes: or thick hung curd drained overnight in muslin

ingredients.name: ripe mangoes, peeled and pureed
ingredients.notes: Alphonso or Kesar give the best flavour; or use 300 ml tinned mango pulp

ingredients.name: agar-agar (china grass) powder
ingredients.notes: level teaspoons; dissolve in cold water before heating

steps.text: Crush the biscuits to fine crumbs in a blender or by placing them in a zip-lock bag and rolling firmly with a glass. Mix with the melted butter until the crumbs are evenly coated and the mixture holds together when pressed.
Press the crumb mixture firmly and evenly into the base of a 20 cm springform tin or a deep round dish lined with cling film. Refrigerate for 20 minutes while you prepare the filling.
Blend the mango flesh to a smooth, lump-free puree. Set aside 4–5 tbsp for the top-layer glaze and reserve the rest for the filling.
Beat the cream cheese until smooth and lump-free. Add condensed milk, lemon juice, and the main portion of mango puree. Beat until the mixture is silky and uniformly coloured.
Whip the heavy cream to soft peaks and fold it gently into the mango–cream-cheese mixture in two additions, using a spatula to keep the batter as airy as possible.

Stir the agar-agar powder into 4 tbsp of cold water in a small saucepan. Soak for 2 minutes, then heat over medium flame, stirring constantly, until the agar dissolves and the liquid becomes clear — about 3 minutes. Do not boil vigorously.

Working quickly while the agar is still warm and fluid, pour it in a thin stream into the cream-cheese mixture, stirring constantly. Pour this filling over the chilled biscuit base and smooth the surface.
Stir the reserved mango puree with 2 tbsp of sugar until the sugar dissolves. Gently spoon it over the filling as an even topping layer.

Refrigerate for at least 4 hours, or overnight, until firmly set. Run a warm knife around the edge before releasing the springform clip or lifting the cling film. Slice with a hot dry knife for the cleanest cuts.

substitutions.substitute: 300 g thick hung curd — drain full-fat dahi in muslin cloth overnight for a lighter, tangier result
150 g Parle-G biscuits mixed with butter — sweeter base with a mild vanilla note
agar-agar bar (china grass stick) — soak 1 full bar in water for 10 minutes, squeeze out, dissolve in the coconut milk over heat
300 ml tinned Alphonso mango pulp (Kesar or Ratna brand) — ideal off-season substitute

culturalNote: No-bake cheesecakes became popular across South and Southeast Asia as a practical adaptation for kitchens without ovens; the mango version is a natural fit for the subcontinent where Alphonso and Kesar mangoes are seasonal summer treasures.
indianKitchenAdaptation: China grass (agar-agar) is sold as flat coloured bars or white powder in most Indian grocery stores and mithaiwalas — the powder is easier to measure and dissolves more evenly. Use Amul cream cheese or thick Amul fresh cream for the filling. During mango season, 2 ripe Alphonsos give the most fragrant result; off-season, Ratna or Kesar tinned pulp is reliable.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Steamed Crème Caramel
description: The classic French custard dessert — silky, trembling, and crowned with a pool of bitter-sweet amber caramel — made entirely on the stovetop using gentle steam instead of a bain-marie in the oven. The result is just as smooth and just as elegant, with no oven required.

ingredients.name: whole milk
ingredients.notes: full-fat gives the silkiest custard

ingredients.name: sweetened condensed milk
ingredients.notes: adds richness; if using, reduce sugar in custard to 2 tbsp

steps.text: Make the caramel: put 6 tbsp sugar and 1 tbsp water in a small saucepan over medium heat. Do not stir — swirl the pan gently if needed. Watch carefully until the liquid turns deep amber and smells toasty, about 5–7 minutes. Immediately pour into four lightly greased ramekins or small steel katoris, tilting each to coat the base. Caramel sets in seconds — work fast.
Warm the milk over low heat in the same or a fresh saucepan until steaming but not boiling. Stir in 3 tbsp sugar and condensed milk until dissolved. Remove from heat and cool for 5 minutes.

Crack the eggs into a bowl and whisk lightly — just enough to break the yolks and combine without beating in air, which would create bubbles in the custard. Pour the warm milk mixture slowly into the beaten eggs while stirring gently. Add vanilla extract, then strain through a fine sieve for the smoothest possible result.
Pour the strained custard gently over the set caramel in each ramekin. Cover each ramekin tightly with aluminium foil to prevent condensation drops from pitting the surface.
Place a trivet or small upturned plate inside a large heavy-bottomed pot or pressure cooker. Pour in enough water to come 2–3 cm deep. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a gentle, steady simmer.

Set the foil-covered ramekins on the trivet. Close the pressure cooker lid WITH the gasket but WITHOUT the whistle weight — you want quiet, gentle steam, not high pressure. Steam on low-to-medium flame for 20–25 minutes until the custard is just set and wobbles slightly in the centre when shaken.
Remove the ramekins carefully and cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 2 hours until the custard is firmly set and the caramel has fully liquefied underneath.

To unmould, run a thin knife around the inside edge, place a deep dessert plate on top, and flip decisively. Lift the ramekin straight up — the caramel will flow down over the sides in a glossy veil. Serve immediately.

substitutions.substitute: 250 ml milk + 150 ml coconut milk for a gentle tropical note in the custard
2 extra tbsp of regular sugar dissolved in the warm milk — equally sweet but less rich
1/4 tsp cardamom powder for an Indian-inflected version — the warm spice pairs beautifully with caramel
this recipe relies on egg proteins to set the custard; for an eggless dessert, see the coconut panna cotta in this collection

culturalNote: Crème caramel is one of the foundational custard desserts of French cuisine and appears with small variations across Spain (flan), Latin America (flan de leche), and the Philippines (leche flan) — testimony to French culinary influence carried through colonial history.
regionalVariation: Spanish flan is typically richer with condensed milk; Filipino leche flan uses only egg yolks for a dense, almost solid texture.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A pressure cooker makes a perfect steamer: trivet inside, 2 cups of water, lid ON with the gasket but whistle weight removed. Steam on medium-low for 22 minutes. Small steel katoris (the kind used for dal) work perfectly in place of ceramic ramekins. Amul full-fat milk gives the richest custard. The caramel technique is identical to making the sugar base for Indian caramel payasam.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Apple Crumble
description: Soft, cinnamon-spiced stewed apples covered with a rough, buttery oat crumble that bakes into a shatteringly crisp golden topping — one of the simplest and most satisfying British puddings, equally good with a scoop of vanilla ice cream or a pour of warm custard.

ingredients.name: apples (any tart variety), peeled, cored, and sliced
ingredients.notes: a mix of tart and sweet apples gives the best depth

ingredients.name: cold butter, cut into small cubes
ingredients.notes: butter must be cold to create a proper crumble texture

steps.text: Preheat the oven to 190°C (375°F). Peel, core, and slice the apples into 1 cm thick wedges. Toss in a bowl with 3 tbsp sugar, 1 tsp cinnamon, and lemon juice until well coated.
Spread the spiced apple slices in an even layer in a 23 cm round or 20×25 cm rectangular ovenproof dish — they should fill the dish about three-quarters full.

Make the crumble: combine the oats, maida, 1/3 cup sugar, and a pinch of salt in a bowl. Add the cold butter cubes and rub them into the dry ingredients with your fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse, clumpy breadcrumbs. Some pea-sized butter lumps are ideal — they bake into crisp flakes. Work quickly so the butter stays cold.
Scatter the crumble loosely and evenly over the apple layer, covering it completely. Do not press it down — the loose texture is precisely what crisps up in the oven.

Bake for 35–40 minutes until the topping is deeply golden and the apple juices bubble vigorously around the edges. If the topping colours too quickly, cover loosely with foil for the final 10 minutes.
Rest for 5 minutes before serving — the filling is extremely hot straight from the oven. Serve warm in deep bowls with vanilla ice cream, warm custard, or cold cream.

substitutions.substitute: ripe pears (naspati) in the same quantity — softer texture, slightly less tart
90 g chilled coconut oil for a dairy-free crumble — rub in very quickly before it warms
1.25 cups maida only (no oats) for a traditional all-flour British crumble — same technique, slightly finer texture
1/2 tsp amchur (dry mango powder) for a similar bright tartness without the citrus

culturalNote: Apple crumble was born out of wartime rationing in Britain when butter and flour were scarce and traditional pastry was a luxury; the simpler crumble topping proved so delicious that it has outlasted the rationing by eighty years.
regionalVariation: Australian crumble often features peaches or stone fruits instead of apples; Scottish cranachan layers oats, whisky cream, and raspberries in a similar oat-forward spirit.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Bake in an OTG at 190°C — most Indian OTGs handle this well. For a no-oven option, stew the apples in a kadai with sugar and cinnamon until just tender, transfer to a heatproof dish, top with the crumble, and toast under the grill setting of an OTG or convection microwave at 190°C for 15 minutes until the topping turns golden. Himachal Pradesh apples (Shimla or Kinnaur variety) available at Indian markets are excellent for this recipe.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Coconut Panna Cotta
description: A silky, ivory-white panna cotta made entirely with coconut milk and set with agar-agar — vegan, dairy-free, and fragrant with vanilla. It wobbles on the plate when unmoulded and pairs beautifully with a spoonful of ripe mango or a drizzle of toasted coconut caramel.

ingredients.name: full-fat coconut milk (tinned)
ingredients.notes: shake the tin before opening; do not use light coconut milk

ingredients.name: coconut cream or thick coconut milk
ingredients.notes: the thick layer from a refrigerated tin works perfectly

ingredients.name: agar-agar (china grass) powder
ingredients.notes: level teaspoons; too much agar gives a stiff, jelly-like set instead of a silky wobble

steps.text: Whisk the agar-agar powder into 4 tbsp of cold water in a small saucepan and let it soak for 2 minutes — this prevents lumping when it hits the heat.

Pour both portions of coconut milk into the saucepan with the soaked agar. Add sugar and a pinch of salt. Place over medium heat and bring to a steady simmer while stirring constantly, until the agar is fully dissolved and the liquid is smooth and lump-free, about 4–5 minutes. Do not rush with high heat.
Remove from heat and stir in vanilla extract. Taste and adjust sweetness — remember the flavour mellows when cold, so it should taste a touch sweeter than your preference.

Pour the mixture through a fine sieve into four lightly greased ramekins, small steel katoris, or silicone moulds. Tap each gently on the counter to release air bubbles. Cool to room temperature, about 20 minutes.
Refrigerate for at least 4 hours until the panna cotta is firmly set throughout — it should hold its shape when unmoulded but still give a gentle wobble when nudged.

To unmould, run a thin knife around the inside of each mould, place a chilled dessert plate on top, and invert with a confident single flip. Lift the mould away. Arrange sliced mango around the base and scatter toasted coconut over the top. Serve immediately.

substitutions.substitute: 400 ml full-fat dairy cream + 200 ml whole milk for a classic non-vegan panna cotta — mark as vegetarian
1 full china grass bar (about 7 g), soaked in water for 10 minutes, squeezed out, and dissolved in the coconut milk over heat
3 tbsp coconut sugar for a caramel note and a slightly golden tint
fresh passion fruit pulp, sliced strawberries, or tinned lychees as equally vibrant tropical accompaniments

culturalNote: Panna cotta — literally 'cooked cream' in Italian — is a Piedmontese dessert that became a global restaurant staple for its elegant simplicity; the coconut milk version is especially popular across South and Southeast Asia where dairy is less common.
regionalVariation: Japanese annin tofu uses almond milk and agar for a similar silky set; Thai coconut pudding (khanom thuay) uses coconut milk in small jasmine-scented cups.
indianKitchenAdaptation: China grass (agar-agar) is sold as flat coloured bars or white powder in most Indian grocery stores and sweet-shop supply outlets — the powder measures more accurately for this recipe. Tinned coconut milk from Thai Kitchen, Real Thai, or domestic brands is available at metro supermarkets. Serve in kulhad cups or small steel katoris for a beautiful presentation; the mango topping makes it feel like a tropical payasam in a new form.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Caramelised French Toast
description: Thick bread slices soaked in a vanilla-scented egg custard, then fried in brown butter until the outside is a shatteringly crisp caramel and the inside remains cloud-soft. Pain perdu — 'lost bread' in French — is the most pleasurable way to rescue stale bread and the best reason to wake up early.

ingredients.name: bread (thick slices, preferably day-old)
ingredients.notes: day-old milk bread or any white bread absorbs the custard without falling apart

ingredients.name: sugar
ingredients.notes: 1 tbsp for the custard soak, 2 tbsp for caramelising on the pan

steps.text: Whisk together the eggs, milk, 1 tbsp sugar, vanilla extract, and cinnamon (if using) in a wide shallow bowl until smooth and uniform — this is the custard soak.

Lay the bread slices into the custard one at a time and let them soak for 30 seconds per side. The bread should be saturated but still holding together. Lift out with a slotted spatula, letting excess drip back into the bowl.

Heat 1 tbsp butter in a wide frying pan or tawa over medium flame. When the butter foams and the foam begins to subside, scatter 1 tbsp of sugar evenly over the pan base — it will begin to caramelise into a thin amber layer almost immediately.

Place 3 soaked bread slices into the pan in a single layer, pressing them gently into the caramel. Fry undisturbed for 2–3 minutes until the underside is deeply golden and the caramel is set and crisp. Flip carefully and cook the second side for 1–2 minutes until golden.
Transfer to a warm plate. Repeat with the remaining butter, sugar, and bread slices — each batch gets a fresh scatter of sugar.
Serve immediately, as the caramel crust softens within minutes. Top with a dusting of icing sugar, fresh fruit, honey, or simply eat them as they are while they are hot and crisp.

substitutions.substitute: thick-sliced brioche or milk pav for an even richer, softer result
2 tbsp ghee for a nutty, aromatic alternative that caramelises beautifully
3 tbsp thick curd thinned with 3 tbsp water — adds a subtle tang
2 tbsp jaggery powder for the caramelising step — gives a deeper, more complex caramel with notes of molasses

culturalNote: Pain perdu — literally 'lost bread' — has been a French household staple since at least the fourteenth century; the name reflects its original purpose of rescuing stale bread that would otherwise be wasted, a principle that remains as practical as ever.
regionalVariation: New Orleans-style French toast is thick-cut, soaked overnight, and served with powdered sugar and syrup; British 'eggy bread' is the same preparation but fried plain without caramelising.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A flat tawa works perfectly for this — the same setup used for making parathas. Use Amul butter or ghee; ghee gives a nuttier caramel that pairs especially well with jaggery. Day-old pav buns from the bakery, split open and soaked for the same time, are an excellent substitute for bread slices. Serve with a drizzle of honey or fresh banana slices for a quick, impressive breakfast.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Fruit Custard
description: A beloved Indian party dessert — smooth, creamy vanilla custard stirred together with a colourful medley of seasonal fruit and served chilled. Quick to make, endlessly customisable, and the dessert most likely to disappear first from any buffet spread.

ingredients.name: whole milk
ingredients.notes: full-fat gives the richest custard

ingredients.name: vanilla custard powder (Bird's or equivalent)
ingredients.notes: or 2 tbsp cornflour + 1 tsp vanilla extract for a homemade version

ingredients.name: vanilla extract (optional, if not in custard powder)
ripe banana, sliced
ingredients.notes: add just before serving to prevent browning

ingredients.name: pineapple chunks
ingredients.notes: fresh or tinned; if using tinned, drain well

steps.text: Set aside 4 tbsp of the cold milk. Pour the remaining milk into a saucepan and place over medium heat. Bring to a gentle simmer, stirring to prevent scorching.

Meanwhile, dissolve the custard powder and sugar in the 4 tbsp of cold milk, whisking until completely smooth and lump-free — this slurry technique prevents the custard from lumping when it hits the hot milk.

Once the milk is simmering, pour the custard slurry into it in a slow, steady stream while stirring constantly. Keep stirring and cook over medium-low heat for 4–6 minutes until the custard thickens enough to coat the back of a spoon — it will thicken further as it cools.
Remove from heat. Press a piece of cling film or a damp cloth directly onto the surface of the custard to prevent a skin from forming. Allow to cool completely to room temperature.
Once the custard is at room temperature (or chilled, if you prepared it ahead), stir it until smooth. Fold in the diced apple, mango, and pineapple chunks.
Add the sliced banana and fold in gently. Serve immediately or cover and refrigerate for up to 2 hours. If refrigerating, add the banana just before serving to keep it from discolouring.

substitutions.substitute: 2 tbsp cornflour (cornstarch) + 1/2 tsp vanilla extract + a pinch of turmeric for colour — makes a perfectly good custard from scratch
full-fat soy milk or oat milk for a dairy-free version — the custard sets slightly thinner
tinned fruit cocktail (drained) in the off-season — a classic shortcut that works perfectly
pomegranate arils or seedless grapes for texture contrast; add at the last moment as they soften quickly

culturalNote: Fruit custard has been a fixture at Indian birthday parties, weddings, and restaurant buffets since the mid-twentieth century, when custard powder was imported from Britain and quickly adopted into the Indian sweet-making repertoire; it is now more Indian than British in character.
regionalVariation: South Indian versions often include jelly cubes (red and yellow) and tinned peaches; Punjabi households may add rabri or condensed milk to the warm custard for extra richness.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Condensed-Milk Chocolate Truffles
description: Silky, fudge-like chocolate balls made by cooking condensed milk and cocoa into a thick ganache that sets firm enough to roll — the South American brigadeiro technique, adopted enthusiastically by Indian kitchens that always have a tin of condensed milk in the pantry. No baking, no eggs, no special equipment.

ingredients.name: desiccated coconut or extra cocoa powder, for rolling
ingredients.notes: cocoa powder, chopped nuts, or hundreds-and-thousands also work

steps.text: Combine condensed milk, cocoa powder, and butter in a heavy-bottomed saucepan over medium-low heat. Stir continuously with a silicone spatula or wooden spoon from the moment the heat goes on — this mixture burns easily if left unattended.

Cook, stirring constantly, for 12–15 minutes until the mixture thickens noticeably, darkens in colour, and begins to pull away cleanly from the sides and base of the pan when you drag the spatula across the bottom. It should hold a brief trail before settling. If you drop a small amount onto a cold plate, it should hold its shape.
Add vanilla extract and a pinch of salt, stir to combine, and remove from heat.

Pour the ganache onto a lightly greased plate or into a shallow bowl and allow it to cool to room temperature — about 20 minutes. Then refrigerate for 1 hour until firm enough to roll into balls without sticking.

Spread the desiccated coconut (or cocoa powder) in a shallow bowl. With lightly greased or wet palms, scoop teaspoon-sized portions of the chilled ganache and roll each into a smooth ball between your palms.

Drop each truffle into the coating bowl and roll until evenly coated. Place in small paper cases or on a lined tray. Refrigerate until ready to serve; they keep for up to 5 days covered in the refrigerator.

substitutions.substitute: coconut condensed milk (Magnum brand or homemade by reducing coconut milk with sugar) for a vegan version — mark allergens accordingly
4 tbsp drinking chocolate powder (Bournvita) — sweeter and less intense; reduce to 3 tbsp if you prefer a milder flavour
2 tbsp coconut oil for a dairy-free version or when making a vegan adaptation
50 g finely chopped roasted peanuts or cashews for a nutty coating — adds crunch and toasty flavour

culturalNote: The Brazilian brigadeiro — named after Brigadeiro Eduardo Gomes, a 1940s presidential candidate — is made from this exact ganache technique, rolled in chocolate sprinkles; the condensed-milk chocolate ball tradition also exists across South America and has been independently beloved in Indian homes for decades wherever Milkmaid tin is kept.
indianKitchenAdaptation: These are tailor-made for an Indian kitchen: heavy kadai or saucepan, condensed milk from a Milkmaid tin, and Cadbury or Amul cocoa powder. The cooking-on-flame technique closely resembles making peda or fudge — stir constantly and trust the process. They make excellent Diwali gifts packed in small boxes.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Nankhatai
description: Crumbly, cardamom-scented Indian shortbread — golden on the outside, melt-in-the-mouth inside, and fragrant with ghee and a hint of nutmeg. An age-old bakery staple from North India that needs only five ingredients and twenty minutes in the oven to become the most loved biscuit in the house.

ingredients.name: ghee, at room temperature (not melted)
ingredients.notes: ghee at a soft, spreadable consistency is essential for creaming; do not melt

ingredients.name: icing sugar (powdered sugar)
ingredients.notes: sieve finely; granulated sugar will not cream smoothly with ghee

steps.text: Preheat oven to 180°C (350°F). Line a baking tray with parchment paper.
Sieve the maida and baking powder together into a bowl and set aside.

Beat the ghee and sifted icing sugar together in a separate bowl using a wooden spoon or hand beater until the mixture turns pale, fluffy, and increases slightly in volume — about 2–3 minutes. This creaming step is what gives nankhatai its characteristic crumbly, short texture.
Add cardamom powder and nutmeg (if using) to the creamed ghee and stir to combine.

Add the sifted flour mixture to the ghee mixture. Bring everything together with your hands into a soft, smooth dough. Do not knead — just press and fold until the flour is fully incorporated. The dough should not be sticky; if it is, refrigerate for 10 minutes.

Pinch off equal portions of dough (about 1 heaped tbsp each) and roll into smooth balls. You should get 15–18 cookies. Place on the lined tray with at least 2 cm space between each, and press each ball down very slightly to a 1.5 cm thick disc.

Press a pistachio into the centre of each disc if using. Bake at 180°C for 18–22 minutes until the base turns light golden-brown. The tops will look pale and underdone — this is correct. Do not overbake; nankhatai firms up dramatically as it cools.

Remove from the oven and leave the cookies on the tray for 10 minutes — they are extremely fragile when hot. Transfer carefully to a rack and cool completely before eating. Store in an airtight tin; they keep at room temperature for up to 10 days.

substitutions.substitute: cold unsalted butter — cream it the same way; the result will be slightly richer and less crumbly than the ghee version
a mix of 3/4 cup maida + 1/4 cup besan for a slightly more crumbly, nuttier nankhatai — common in Gujarati versions
1/2 tsp fennel (saunf) powder for a milder, anise-scented version
whole cashew halves or slivered almonds pressed into the centre — equally traditional

culturalNote: Nankhatai — from the Persian 'nan' (bread) and 'khatai' (biscuit) — has Mughal origins and became a staple of North Indian and Pakistani bakeries (naanbaais); the biscuit is an essential Diwali and Eid offering across the subcontinent.
regionalVariation: Gujarati nankhatai often include besan (gram flour) for extra crumbliness; Karachi-style versions from Pakistan are sometimes made with dalda (vanaspati) and are slightly thicker and chewier.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Nankhatai is inherently Indian — no adaptation needed. If you lack an oven, use the pressure cooker tin method: spread a 2 cm layer of salt in the cooker base, place a steel stand, preheat the cooker on medium flame for 10 minutes (lid on, no gasket, no whistle), arrange cookies in a greased steel plate on the stand, and bake on low flame for 20–25 minutes until the base turns golden. Check from 18 minutes.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen
